CHUNK-01 | Core Concept & Definition
TAGS: virtual-farming, definition, overview
Q1: What is Virtual Farming?
Virtual Farming allows people to own real livestock animals while professional farmers manage the
farming process using modern technology. It is a technology-enabled agriculture pa

modern technology. It is a technology-enabled agriculture participation model
that lets urban individuals own real farming assets remotely, without physically managing farms.
Q2: What is Managed Livestock Farming?
Managed Livestock Farming is a model where livestock ownership and farm operations are separated.
Asset owners purchase real animals, an

ations are separated.
Asset owners purchase real animals, and professional farm managers handle all rearing, feeding,
veterinary care, and monitoring using scientific methods — so the owner does not need to be physically
present.
Q3: Is this real farming or a digital simulation?
This is real farming. Each participant owns a real livestock animal ra

al farming. Each participant owns a real livestock animal raised on farms. The digital platform
only helps track and manage the livestock — it is not a simulation.
Q4: What problem does Virtual Farming solve?
Urban people want agriculture exposure but lack land and expertise. Farmers need capital, technology,
and market access. Virtual Farming conn

capital, technology,
and market access. Virtual Farming connects urban asset owners with rural farmers through
technology, solving both problems at once.
CHUNK-02 | Business Model & How It Works
TAGS: business-model, booking, revenue
Q5: How does the business model work?
Three components: (1) Livestock asset ownership by participants, (2) Farm mana

(1) Livestock asset ownership by participants, (2) Farm management services by
the company, (3) Market linkage for livestock sale. Participants book a goat or sheep, become legal
owners, and the company manages rearing for ~6 months after which owners choose their exit.
Q6: Who owns the livestock?
The participant owns the livestock from the date of

vestock?
The participant owns the livestock from the date of purchase. The company only provides farm
management services — ownership is fully with the participant.
Q7: Are funds pooled together?
No. Each purchase corresponds to a specific animal with a unique identification. Funds are never
pooled. This is not a pooled investment scheme. This is a

er
pooled. This is not a pooled investment scheme. This is an individual asset ownership model with a
professional farm management service layered on top.
Q8: Is this a financial investment product?
No. This is a livestock purchase and farm management model. Participants purchase real animals
rather than investing in a financial instrument. No fixe

als
rather than investing in a financial instrument. No fixed returns are promised and no referral chain is
applicable.

Q9: How are the animals monitored?
Animals are monitored through unique ear tags or QR tags, farm records, health monitoring systems,
and AI-base

R tags, farm records, health monitoring systems,
and AI-based livestock monitoring tools. Each animal has a traceable identity throughout the cycle,
along with real-time geo-location tracking.
Q10: Are veterinarians involved?
Yes. Veterinary professionals supervise animal health, vaccination, and disease management
throughout the 6-month farming cy

on, and disease management
throughout the 6-month farming cycle.
Q11: What is the expected weight gain?
Goats typically grow from 15–18 kg at booking to 25–30 kg over 6 months. Actual growth may vary
depending on animal health and environmental conditions.
Q12: What does the goat eat?
Animals receive a scientifically balanced diet including green f

als receive a scientifically balanced diet including green fodder, dry fodder, and nutritional
supplements. Farm staff handle daily feeding, cleaning, and monitoring.

TAGS: AI, blockchain, monitoring, transparency
Q13: How does AI help in livestock farming?
AI systems help monitor growth trends, feed ef

tock farming?
AI systems help monitor growth trends, feed efficiency, health indicators, and productivity forecasts. AI
also predicts mortality risk and market readiness, enabling proactive farm management.
Q14: Why use blockchain?
Blockchain helps maintain tamper-proof records of animal identity, ownership, farm history, and health
records — ensur

dentity, ownership, farm history, and health
records — ensuring full transparency for asset owners.
Q15: How do participants track their animals?
Participants receive an animal identification number, farm updates, growth records, and health reports.
All records are maintained on the digital platform with blockchain-backed transparency.

l platform with blockchain-backed transparency.

AI livestock monitoring, blockchain livestock records, and farm management data form the core

CHUNK-05 | Exit Options & Resale
TAGS: exit, resale, delivery, completion
Q17: What happens after 6 months?
Participants have three o

Q17: What happens after 6 months?
Participants have three options: (1) Take delivery of the animal from the farm, (2) Nominate a buyer and
transfer ownership, (3) Sell the animal back to the company at market price x live weight.
Q18: Can I take delivery of my goat?
Yes. Participants may collect the animal from the farm or request transportation a

collect the animal from the farm or request transportation arrangements.
Q19: How is resale price calculated?
Resale Price = Live Weight (kg) x Local Market Price (Rs./kg). The heavier the animal and the higher
the market rate, the better the resale value.
Q20: Can I sell the goat privately?
Yes. Options include selling privately to a nominated buy

y?
Yes. Options include selling privately to a nominated buyer of your choice, or back to the company at
market price.
CHUNK-06 | Risk Management
TAGS: risk, mortality, insurance, mitigation
Q21: Are returns guaranteed?
No. Livestock farming outcomes depend on natural conditions such as animal health and market prices.
Returns are market-linked and

imal health and market prices.
Returns are market-linked and cannot be guaranteed.
Q22: What happens if the animal dies / goat dies?
If mortality occurs due to proven negligence by the company, the company will provide a replacement
animal or a full refund according to policy. For natural mortality, outcomes depend on insurance
coverage — refund el

mortality, outcomes depend on insurance
coverage — refund eligibility is subject to the insurance terms applicable at the time. In all cases, the
company will communicate promptly with the participant.
Q23: How are risks minimised?
Risks are minimised through veterinary supervision, scientific feeding, farm hygiene protocols, AI-based
disease predi

ific feeding, farm hygiene protocols, AI-based
disease prediction, mortality detection, and livestock insurance where applicable.

Q27: Why goat farming?
Goat meat demand is high due to cultural preferences, religious festivals, and rising protein de

ural preferences, religious festivals, and rising protein demand.
Goats have a relatively low mortality risk, a short 6-month cycle, and are highly scalable through
contract farmers and SHGs.
Q28: Is goat meat demand increasing?
Yes. Demand is increasing due to population growth and rising protein consumption across India.

protein consumption across India.
Q29: What is the market size?
India has one of the largest livestock populations in the world, making the sector highly scalable. Goat
meat (chevon/mutton) enjoys strong cultural and religious demand year-round.

CHUNK-16 | Comparison — Virtual Farming vs Other Assets
TAGS: comparison, FD, stocks, investment
Q64: Is Virtual Farming b

comparison, FD, stocks, investment
Q64: Is Virtual Farming better than a Fixed Deposit?
They are different asset types. A Fixed Deposit guarantees returns; Virtual Farming involves a real
tangible asset (goat) with market-linked outcomes. No fixed return is guaranteed in livestock farming,
but you own a real asset.
Q65: Why choose Virtual Farming o

but you own a real asset.
Q65: Why choose Virtual Farming over stocks?
Virtual Farming offers a tangible real-world asset with direct rural impact. It is not linked to stock market
volatility. Outcomes depend on livestock performance and market prices.
Q66: Is this similar to a chit fund or NBFC scheme?
No. Funds are not pooled, and no fixed retur

or NBFC scheme?
No. Funds are not pooled, and no fixed returns are promised. Each participant owns a specific,
individually identified animal. This is a livestock ownership model, not a financial scheme.

r Tag or QR Tag?

ons, partnering with

A rural farmer who partners with Uzhavar Sandhai to rear animals on behalf of asset owners on their

The risk of an animal dying due to disease, accident, or natural causes during the farming cycle.

CHUNK-19 | Seasonal Demand & Pricing Context
TAGS: seasonal, festival, pricing, Bakrid, wedding
Q76: When is the best time to sell my goat?
Demand and prices are typically highest during Eid al-Adha (Bakrid) and the wedding season
(Nov–Feb). Timing your 6-mon

(Bakrid) and the wedding season
(Nov–Feb). Timing your 6-month cycle to end near these periods may improve resale value, though no
specific return is guaranteed.
Q77: Does the company help with market timing?
Yes. The farm management team monitors market conditions and provides guidance on optimal exit
timing based on live weight and current market

optimal exit
timing based on live weight and current market rates.
Q78: How is the goat price determined at sale?
Price = Live Weight (kg) x Prevailing Local Market Rate (Rs./kg). Market rates fluctuate based on
season, demand, and region.
Q79: Which festivals drive peak goat prices?
Eid al-Adha (Bakrid) is the largest single demand event. Other p

al-Adha (Bakrid) is the largest single demand event. Other peak periods include Diwali/Navratri,
Pongal/Sankranti, wedding season (Nov–Feb), and Ramadan.

1. Introduction
These Terms govern all bookings made by any buyer ("Buyer") with Uzhavar Sandhai Pvt Ltd ("Company")
for the advance purchase and rearing of live animals under the Company's farm management pr

aring of live animals under the Company's farm management program.

This electronic acceptance shall have the same legal effect as a manually signed agreement under the

Uzha

Information Technology Act, 2000.
2. About the Company
Uzhavar Sandhai Pvt Ltd is a recognized agri-startup incubated and supported by The Ministry of
Agriculture & Farmers Welfare, Govt. of India (IEF of CCS NIAM).
The Company operates a technology-enabled precision livestock contract farming model using AI-based
monitoring, blockchain-linked rec

rming model using AI-based
monitoring, blockchain-linked records, and scientifically managed rearing practices to ensure transparency
and higher productivity for farmers and buyers.

under real farm conditions.

eal farm conditions.
3. Nature of Transaction
This is a purchase of a specific live animal, not an investment or deposit.
Each Rs. 6,000–10,000 booking corresponds to one goat of approximately 15–18 kg at the time of booking
based on breed and animal.
The Company will rear the animal for approximately six (6) months, targeting a healthy growth to 2

pproximately six (6) months, targeting a healthy growth to 25–30 kg
(subject to natural variations).
The Buyer retains ownership of the animal during this period. The Company provides custodial care and
rearing services only.
4. Booking & Ownership
Upon payment, the Buyer will receive a Booking ID linked to a uniquely tagged animal (ear tag or QR).

oking ID linked to a uniquely tagged animal (ear tag or QR).
Ownership vests with the Buyer from the date of booking; the Company holds the animal in trust and
maintains full farm and health records.
The Company ensures proper feed, veterinary care, and safe housing throughout the rearing period.
5. Completion Options after Six (6–36) Months
At the

period.
5. Completion Options after Six (6–36) Months
At the end of the rearing period, the Buyer may choose any one of the following options:
1. Delivery Option – Take delivery of the live animal at the Company's farm or request transport
(charges extra).
2. Nomination Option – Nominate a third-party buyer to collect the animal directly.
3. Resale

third-party buyer to collect the animal directly.
3. Resale Back Option – Offer to sell the animal back to the Company. The resale value will be
calculated as: Actual live weight x prevailing local mandi market rate on the date of resale. The
Company may, at its discretion, buy back the animal based on demand and farm capacity.
